title: Neural Structured Prediction for Inductive Node Classification

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## NEURAL STRUCTURED PREDICTION FOR INDUCTIVE NODE CLASSIFICATION

1Mila - Qu´ebec AI Institute

2Universit´e de Montr´eal

3HEC Montr´eal

4Canadian Institute for Advanced Research (CIFAR)

## ABSTRACT

This paper studies node classification in the inductive setting, i.e., aiming to learn a model on labeled training graphs and generalize it to infer node labels on unlabeled test graphs. This problem has been extensively studied with graph neural networks (GNNs) by learning effective node representations, as well as traditional structured prediction methods for modeling the structured output of node labels, e.g., conditional random fields (CRFs). In this paper, we present a new approach called the Structured Proxy Network (SPN), which combines the advantages of both worlds. SPN defines flexible potential functions of CRFs with GNNs. However, learning such a model is nontrivial as it involves optimizing a maximin game with high-cost inference. Inspired by the underlying connection between joint and marginal distributions defined by Markov networks, we propose to solve an approximate version of the optimization problem as a proxy, which yields a nearoptimal solution, making learning more efficient. Extensive experiments on two settings show that our approach outperforms many competitive baselines[1] .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graph-structured data are ubiquitous in the real world, covering a variety of applications. This paper studies node classification, a fundamental problem in the machine learning community. Most existing efforts focus on the transductive setting (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Velickoviˇ c et al., 2018), i.e., using´ a small set of labeled nodes in a graph to classify the rest of nodes. In this paper, we study node classification in the inductive setting (Hamilton et al., 2017), which is receiving growing interest. Given some training graphs with all nodes labeled, we aim to classify nodes in unlabeled test graphs.

This problem has been recently studied with graph neural networks (GNNs) (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017; Velickoviˇ c et al., 2018).´ GNNs infer the marginal label distribution of each node by learning useful node representations based on node features and edges. Once a GNN is learned on training graphs, it can be further applied to test graphs to infer node labels. Owing to the high capacity of nonlinear neural architectures, GNNs achieve impressive results on many datasets. However, one limitation of GNNs is that they ignore the joint dependency of node labels, and therefore node labels are predicted separately without modeling structured output.

Indeed, modeling structured output has been widely explored by the literature of structured prediction (BakIr et al., 2007). Structured prediction methods predict node labels collectively, so the label prediction of each node can be improved according to the predicted labels of neighboring nodes. One representative approach is the conditional random field (CRF) (Lafferty et al., 2001). A CRF models the joint distribution of node labels with Markov networks, and thus training CRFs becomes a learning task in graphical models, while predicting node labels corresponds to an inference task. Typically, the potential functions in CRFs are parameterized as log-linear functions, which suffer from low model capacities. One remedy for this is to define potential functions with GNNs (Ma et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2019). However, most of the effective methods for learning CRFs involve a

maximin game (Wainwright & Jordan, 2008; Sutton & McCallum, 2012), making learning often hard to converge, especially when GNNs are used to parameterize potential functions. Besides, as learning CRFs requires doing inference on the graphical models, the combined model requires a long run time.

In this paper, we address these challenges by proposing SPN (Structured Proxy Network), which is high in capacity, efficient in learning, and able to model the joint dependency of node labels. SPN is inspired by theoretical works in graphical models (Wainwright & Jordan, 2008), which reveal close connections between the joint label distribution and the node/edge marginal label distribution in a Markov network. Based on that, we approximate the original optimization problem with a proxy problem, where the potential functions in CRFs are defined by combining a collection of node/edge pseudomarginal distributions, which are parameterized by GNNs that satisfy a few simple constraints. This proxy problem can be easily solved by maximizing the data likelihood on each node and edge, which yields a near-optimal joint label distribution on training graphs. Once the model is learned, we apply it to test graphs and run loopy belief propagation (Murphy et al., 1999) to infer node labels. Experiments on two settings against both GNNs and CRFs prove the effectiveness of our approach.

Note that although SPN is tested on inductive node classification, this method is quite general and can be applied to many other structured prediction tasks as well, such as POS tagging (Church, 1988) and named entity recognition (Sang & De Meulder, 2003). Please refer to Sec. 4.3 for more details.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Graph neural networks (GNNs) perform node classification by learning useful node representations (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017; Velickoviˇ c et al., 2018).´ Most earlier efforts focus on designing GNNs for transductive node classification (Yang et al., 2016; Gao & Ji, 2019; Xhonneux et al., 2020), and many recent works move to the inductive setting (Hamilton et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2018; Chiang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020a; Zeng et al., 2020). Because of high capacity and efficient training, GNNs achieve impressive results on inductive node classification. Despite the success, GNNs only try to model the marginal distribution of each node label and predict node labels separately without considering joint dependency. In contrast, SPN models joint distributions of node labels with CRFs, which predicts node labels collectively to improve results.

Another type of approach for inductive node classification is structured prediction, which focuses on modeling the dependency of node labels, so that the predicted node labels are more consistent. One representative approach is structured SVM (Tsochantaridis et al., 2005; Finley & Joachims, 2008; Sarawagi & Gupta, 2008), but it lacks a probabilistic interpretation to handle the uncertainty of the prediction. Another representative probabilistic approach is conditional random field (Lafferty et al., 2001; Sutton & McCallum, 2006), which models the distribution of output spaces by using a Markov network. CRFs have been proven effective in many applications, such as POS tagging (Lafferty et al., 2001), shallow parsing (Sha & Pereira, 2003), image labeling (He et al., 2004), and sequence labeling (Lample et al., 2016; Ma & Hovy, 2016; Liu et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the potential functions in CRFs are typically defined as log-linear functions, suffering from low model capacity.

There are also some recent works trying to combine GNNs and CRFs. Some works use GNNs to solve inference problems in graphical models (Dai et al., 2016; Satorras et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b; Satorras & Welling, 2020). In contrast, our approach uses GNNs to parameterize the potential functions in CRFs, which is in a similar vein to Ma et al. (2018); Qu et al. (2019); Ma et al. (2019; 2021); Wang et al. (2021). Among them, Ma et al. (2018) and Qu et al. (2019) optimize the pseudolikelihood (Besag, 1975) for model learning, and Wang et al. (2021) optimizes a cross-entropy loss on each single node, which can yield poor approximation of the true joint likelihood (Koller & Friedman, 2009; Sutton & McCallum, 2012). Our approach instead solves a proxy problem, which yields a near-optimal solution to the original problem of maximizing likelihood, and thus gets superior results. For Ma et al. (2019) and Ma et al. (2021), they focus on transductive node classification and continuous labels respectively, which are different from our work.

Lastly, learning CRFs has also been widely studied. Some works solve a maximin game as a surrogate for learning (Sutton & McCallum, 2012) and some others maximize a lower bound of the likelihood function (Sutton & McCallum, 2009). However, these maximin games are often hard to optimize and the lower bounds are often loose. Different from them, we follow Wainwright et al. (2003) and build an approximate optimization problem as a proxy, which is easier to solve and yields better results.

This paper focuses on inductive node classification (Hamilton et al., 2017), a fundamental problem in both graph machine learning and structured prediction. We employ a probabilistic formalization for the problem with some labeled training graphs and unlabeled test graphs. Each training graph is given as ( **y** _V[∗][,]_ **[ x]** _[V][ , E]_[)][, where] **[ x]** _[V]_[and] **[ y]** _V[∗]_[are features and labels of a set of nodes] _[ V]_[ , and] _[ E]_[is a set of] edges. For each test graph ( **x** _V_ ˜ _, E_[˜] ), only features **x** _V_ ˜ and edges _E_[˜] are given. Then we aim to solve:

• **Learning.** On training graphs, learn a probabilistic model to approximate _p_ ( **y** _V |_ **x** _V , E_ ). • **Inference.** For each test graph, infer node labels **y** _V[∗]_ ˜[according to the distribution] _[ p]_[(] **[y]** _V_[ ˜] _[|]_ **[x]** _V_[ ˜] _[,][E]_[˜][)][.]
The problem has been extensively studied in both graph machine learning and structured prediction fields, and representative methods are GNNs and CRFs respectively. Next, we introduce the details.

## 3.1 GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

For inductive node classification, graph neural networks (GNNs) learn node representations to predict marginal label distributions of nodes. GNNs assume all node labels are independent conditioned on node features and edges, so the joint label distribution is factorized into a set of marginals as below:

Each marginal distribution _pθ_ ( _ys|_ **x** _V , E_ ) is modeled as a categorical distribution over label candidates, and the label probabilities are computed by applying a linear softmax classifier to the representation of node _s_ . In general, node representations are learned via the message passing mechanism (Gilmer et al., 2017), which brings high capacity to GNNs. Also, owing to the factorization in Eq. (1), learning and inference can be easily solved in GNNs, where we simply need to compute loss and make prediction on each node separately. However, GNNs approximate only the marginal label distributions of nodes on training graphs, which may generalize badly and result in poor approximation of node marginal label distributions on test graphs. Also, the labels of different nodes are separately predicted according to their own marginal label distributions, yet the joint dependency of node labels is ignored.

On the one hand, learning relies on inference, meaning that we have to update _{qs_ ( _ys_ ) _}s∈V_ , _{qst_ ( _ys, yt_ ) _}_ ( _s,t_ ) _∈E_ to approximate the node and edge marginals of _pθ_ at each step, which can be expensive. On the other hand, as learning involves a maximin game and the optimal _q_ of the inner minimization problem in Eq. (3) is intractable, we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for _θ_ , making learning unstable. The problem becomes even more severe when _θ_ is parameterized by highly nonlinear neural models, e.g. GNNs.

## 4 MODEL

In this section, we introduce our proposed approach Structured Proxy Network (SPN). The general idea of SPN is to combine GNNs and CRFs by parameterizing potential functions in CRFs with GNNs, and therefore SPN enjoys high capacity and can model the joint dependency of node labels.

However, as elaborated in Sec. 3.2, learning such a model on training graphs is challenging due to the maximin game in optimization. Inspired by the connection between the joint and marginal distributions of CRFs, we instead construct a new optimization problem, which serves as a proxy for model learning. Compared with the original maximin game, the proxy problem is much easier to solve, where we can simply train two GNNs to approximate the marginal label distributions on nodes and edges, and further combine these pseudomarginals (defined in Prop. 1) into a near-optimal joint label distribution. This joint label distribution can be further refined by optimizing the maximin game, although it is optional and often unnecessary, as this distribution is often close enough to the optimal one. With this proxy problem for model learning, learning becomes more stable and efficient.
Afterwards, the learned model is used to predict the joint label distribution on test graphs. Then we run loopy belief propagation to infer node labels. Now, we introduce the details of our approach.

## 4.1 LEARNING

The learning task aims at training _θ_ to maximize the log-likelihood function log _pθ_ ( **y** _V[∗][|]_ **[x]** _[V][ , E]_[)] for each training graph ( **y** _V[∗][,]_ **[ x]** _[V][ , E]_[)][,][which][is][highly][challenging.][Therefore,][instead][of][directly] optimizing this goal, we solve an approximate version of the problem as a proxy, which is training a node GNN and an edge GNN to maximize the log-likelihood of observed labels on nodes and edges.

## 4.3 DISCUSSION

In practice, many structured prediction problems can be viewed as special cases of inductive node classification, where the graphs between nodes have some special structures. For example in sequence labeling tasks (e.g., named entity recognition), the graphs between nodes have sequential structures. Thus, SPN can be applied to these tasks as well. In order for better results, one might replace GNNs with other neural models which are specifically designed for the studied task to better estimate the pseudomarginals. For example in sequence labeling tasks, recurrent neural networks can be used.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

## 5.1 DATASETS

We consider datasets in two settings, which focus on node-level and graph-level accuracy respectively.

**Node-level Accuracy.** The node-level accuracy measures _how likely a model can predict the correct label of a node in test graphs_ . We use the **PPI** dataset (Zitnik & Leskovec, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017), which has 20 training graphs. To make the dataset more challenging, we also try using only the first 1/2/10 training graphs, yielding another three datasets **PPI-1** , **PPI-2** , and **PPI-10** . Besides, we also build a **DBLP** dataset from the citation network in Tang et al. (2008). Papers from eight conferences are treated as nodes, and we split them into three categories for classification according to conference domains[2] . For each paper, we compute the mean GloVe embedding (Pennington et al., 2014) of words in the title and abstract as node features. The training/validation/test graph is formed as the citation graph of papers published before 1999, from 2000 to 2009, after 2010 respectively.

**Graph-level Accuracy.** The graph-level accuracy measures _how likely a model can correctly classify all the nodes for a given test graph_ . We construct three datasets from the Cora, Citeseer, and Pubmed datasets used for transductive node classification (Yang et al., 2016). Each raw dataset has a single graph. For each training/validation/test node of the raw dataset, we treat its ego network[3] as a training/validation/test graph. We denote the datasets as **Cora*** , **Citeseer*** , **Pubmed*** .

## 5.2 COMPARED ALGORITHMS

**Graph Neural Networks.** For GNNs, we choose a few well-known model architectures for comparison, including GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017), GraphSage (Hamilton et al., 2017), GAT (Velickoviˇ c´ et al., 2018), Graph U-Net (Gao & Ji, 2019) and GCNII (Chen et al., 2020a).

**Conditional Random Fields.** For CRFs, we consider three variants. (1) _CRF-linear._ This variant uses linear _θ_ -functions in Eq. (2), which takes the features on nodes and edges for computation. (2) _CRF-GNN._ This variant parameterizes the _θ_ -functions as _θs_ ( _ys_ ) = _f_ ( **u** _s_ ) and _θst_ ( _ys, yt_ ) = _g_ ( **v** _s,_ **v** _t_ ), with _f_ and _g_ defined in Eq. (7) and Eq. (8), where the node representations are generated by different GNN architectures (e.g., CRF-GAT). We train these models via the maximin game as in Eq. (3) with sum-product loopy belief propagation. (3) _GMNN._ We also consider GMNN (Qu et al., 2019), an approach combining GNNs and CRFs, which optimizes the pseudolikelihood function for learning.

**Our Approach.** For SPNs, we try different GNN architectures for defining the node and edge GNNs (e.g., SPN-GAT). By default, we only solve the proxy problem without performing refinement. We systematically compare the results with and without refinement in part 2 of Sec. 5.5.

## 5.3 EVALUATION METRICS

On Cora*, Citeseer*, and Pubmed*, we report the percentage of test graphs where all the nodes are correctly classified (i.e., graph-level accuracy). On DBLP and PPI, we report accuracy and micro-F1 based on the percentage of test nodes which are correctly classified (i.e., node-level accuracy). For Cora*, Citeseer*, and Pubmed*, we run each compared method with 10 different seeds to report the mean accuracy and the standard deviation. For DBLP and PPI, we run each method with 5 seeds.

## 5.4 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

For GNNs, by default we use the same architectures (e.g., number of neurons, number of layers) as used in the original papers. Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) is used for training. For the edge GNN in Eq. (8), we add a hyperparameter _γ_ to control the annealing temperature of the logit _g_ ( **v** _s,_ **v** _t_ ) before the softmax function during belief propagation. Empirically, we find that max-product belief propagation works better than the sum-product variant in most cases, so we use the max-product version by default. By default, we do not run refinement when training SPNs. See Sec. F for details.

## 5.5 RESULTS

**1. Comparison with other methods.** The main results in the two settings are presented in Tab. 1 and Tab. 2. Compared against different GNN models, our approach achieves consistent improvement (the relative underperformance of SPN-GCN and SPN-SAGE is related to the capacity of the backbone GNNs and is explained in Sec. G.1) by using these GNNs as backbone networks for approximating marginal label distributions on nodes and edges, which demonstrates SPNs are able to model the structured output of node labels by combining with CRFs, and thus achieve better results.

Besides, SPNs also achieve superior results to CRF-GNNs which are trained by directly solving the maximin game in Eq. (3), as well as GMNN which optimizes the pseudolikelihood function. This observation proves the advantage of our proposed proxy optimization problem for learning CRFs.

**2. Effect of refinement.** By solving the proxy optimization problem in Eq. (9), we can obtain a near-optimal joint label distribution on training graphs, based on which we may optionally refine the distribution with the maximin game in Eq. (3). Next, we study the effect of refinement, and we present the results in Tab. 4. By only solving the proxy problem, our approach already achieves impressive results, showing that the proxy problem can well approximate the original learning problem. Only on datasets with sufficient labeled data (e.g., PPI-10, PPI), refinement leads to some improvement.

**3. Model architecture.** SPN uses a node GNN and an edge GNN for computing node and edge marginals independently. In practice, we can also use a shared GNN for both node and edge marginals. We show results of this variant in Tab. 6, where it also achieves significant improvement over GNNs.
**4. Efficiency comparison.** We have seen SPNs achieve better classification results than GNNs and CRFs. Next, we further compare their efficiency by showing the run time on DBLP and PPI. For PPI,

**5. Comparison of learning methods.** Next, we investigate different methods for learning SPNs, including directly solving the maximin game, optimizing pseudolikelihood, and solving our proposed proxy problem. We show the results for optimizing SPN-GAT in Tab. 5. We see solving maximin game yields poor results due to unstable training. Although the pseudolikelihood method performs much better, the result is still unsatisfactory as it is not a good approximation of the true likelihood. By solving our proposed proxy problem, SPN achieves the best result, which proves its effectiveness.

**6. Convergence analysis.** To better illustrate the advantage of the proxy problem for learning CRFs, we look into the training curves of SPNs, SPNs w/o proxy, and CRFs when optimizing the maximin game in Eq. (3). For SPNs, we optimize the node and edge GNNs on the proxy optimization problem in Eq. (9) before doing refinement with the maximin game, while for SPNs w/o proxy we directly perform refinement with the maximin game without solving the proxy problem. We show the results in Fig. 2. CRFs and SPNs w/o proxy suffer from high variance and low accuracy. In contrast, owing to the near-optimal joint distribution found by solving the proxy problem, SPNs get much higher accuracy with lower variance even without refinement (see initial results of SPNs at epoch 0). Also, the refinement process quickly converges after only a few epochs, showing good efficiency of SPNs.

**7. Case study.** To intuitively see how SPNs outperform GNNs, we conduct some case studies on Cora*. We use GAT as backbone networks, and show the prediction made by the GAT (the node GNN), the edge GNN, and SPN in Fig. 3. In all three cases shown in the figure, GAT (left column) makes inconsistent predictions on linked nodes, as it fails to model the structured output. The edge GNN (middle column) also makes a mistake in the bottom case. Finally, by combining GAT and edge GNN with a CRF, the SPN (right column) is able to predict the correct labels for all nodes.

## 6 CONCLUSION

This paper studied inductive node classification, and we proposed SPN to combine GNNs and CRFs. Inspired by the connection of joint and marginal distributions defined by Markov networks, we designed a proxy problem for efficient model learning. In the future, we plan to explore more advanced GNNs to model the pseudomarginals on edges, which are key to improving node classification results in SPNs. In addition, SPNs model joint dependency of node labels by defining potential functions on nodes and edges, and we also plan to further explore high-order local structures, e.g., triangles.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel and conceptually interesting approach—Structured Proxy Network (SPN)—that combines GNNs and CRFs for inductive node classification by leveraging pseudomarginals and a proxy learning objective. The motivation is strong: avoiding the instability of maximin optimization in CRF-GNNs while retaining structured prediction benefits. The empirical results show consistent gains over strong baselines, and the efficiency advantage over full CRF training is compelling. The work is well-positioned in the literature and addresses a relevant gap in structured inductive learning.

Overview issues:

- Proxy objective lacks validation of approximation quality: The core theoretical claim is that the proxy problem yields a 'near-optimal' solution to the original likelihood maximization, but this claim is not empirically or analytically substantiated. The paper assumes that matching pseudomarginals to observed labels (via Eq. 10) leads to satisfaction of the moment-matching conditions (Eq. 4), yet there is no measurement of how close the resulting joint distribution is to the true maximum likelihood estimate. For instance, the paper could report the log-likelihood of the proxy solution under the full CRF model, or compare marginals before and after refinement. Without such analysis, the 'near-optimality' remains an unverified assumption, weakening the theoretical contribution.

- Inconsistency in pseudomarginal constraints is ignored without justification: The method relies on pseudomarginals τ_s and τ_st that must satisfy marginalization constraints (Eq. 9), yet the paper states these are 'ignored during optimization' because they 'will almost naturally satisfy the consistency constraint'. This is highly questionable—neural outputs from separate node and edge GNNs are unlikely to satisfy ∑_{y_s} τ_st(y_s, y_t) = τ_t(y_t) without explicit enforcement. The claim that constrained optimization 'yields no improvement' is insufficient; if the constraints are critical for Proposition 1 to hold, violating them could invalidate the entire proxy construction. The paper should either enforce consistency (e.g., via normalization layers) or demonstrate empirically that violations are negligible.

- Generalization to test graphs assumes label dependency structure is preserved: The method learns pseudomarginals on training graphs and applies the same CRF structure to test graphs, assuming that label co-occurrence patterns (captured by edge pseudomarginals) generalize. However, in the inductive setting across graphs, community structure, label homophily, or dependency strength may differ significantly. The paper does not evaluate whether the edge GNN learns graph-invariant dependency patterns or simply overfits to training graph statistics. A critical test would be to evaluate performance when test graphs have different label correlation structures (e.g., via synthetic perturbations), which is missing.

- Inference efficiency claims lack scalability analysis: While Table 3 reports training time, the inference cost of loopy belief propagation (LBP) on large test graphs is not discussed. LBP complexity grows with graph size and label space, and max-product LBP (used by default) may require many iterations to converge. The paper claims efficiency over CRFs, but if SPN uses the same inference procedure, the gain is only in training. For real-world scalability, the paper should report inference time or iterations to convergence on larger graphs, especially since GNNs typically offer O(n) inference while LBP can be much slower.

- Ablation studies do not isolate structured modeling contribution: The ablation in Table 6 compares shared vs. separate GNNs, but does not test whether the CRF integration itself is beneficial. A key missing baseline is a GNN that directly predicts edge label pairs (i.e., a model that maximizes log τ_st(y*_s, y*_t) like SPN but without constructing a joint distribution). If such a model performs similarly, the gains may stem from multitask learning on edges rather than structured prediction. The current ablation cannot distinguish between architectural improvements and genuine modeling of label dependencies.

- Refinement step contradicts the premise of proxy sufficiency: The paper argues the proxy problem yields a near-optimal solution, making refinement 'optional and often unnecessary'. Yet Table 4 shows measurable gains from refinement, especially in high-data regimes. This suggests the proxy is not as close to optimal as claimed. If refinement consistently helps, then the method is effectively a hybrid that still depends on solving part of the maximin game—undermining the core claim of avoiding difficult optimization. The paper should reconcile this by either downplaying the 'near-optimality' or showing that refinement is only a minor correction.

- No measurement of approximation gap in proxy objective: The paper claims the proxy problem yields a 'near-optimal' solution to the original CRF learning objective, but this claim is never quantified. To validate the approximation quality, the authors should report the true log-likelihood of the learned model under the full joint distribution (Eq. 2) before and after refinement, and compare it to a strong baseline like pseudolikelihood or a converged maximin solver on small graphs. Without such a comparison, readers cannot assess whether the proxy actually achieves high-quality solutions or merely sidesteps a hard problem at the cost of statistical efficiency. A concrete demonstration on a small synthetic graph where the partition function can be computed exactly would suffice to measure the gap.

- Missing ablation isolating structured prediction benefit: The performance gains of SPN could stem from either (a) modeling joint label dependencies via the CRF or (b) auxiliary learning from edge-level supervision. The paper fails to disentangle these effects. A critical missing baseline is a GNN that directly maximizes the edge and node log-likelihoods (i.e., the same objective as SPN in Eq. 10) but makes independent predictions at test time—this would test whether the CRF construction adds value beyond multitask training. If this baseline performs comparably, the improvement is not due to structured prediction. The authors must include such a control to support their core claim of combining 'the advantages of both worlds'.

- No evidence pseudomarginal constraints are satisfied: Proposition 1 requires pseudomarginals to satisfy consistency constraints (e.g., ∑_{y_s} τ_st(y_s, y_t) = τ_t(y_t)), yet the model uses separate GNNs for node and edge outputs with no mechanism to enforce this. The claim that constraints are 'almost naturally satisfied' is implausible and unsupported. The authors should report the average L1 violation of these constraints on a validation set—e.g., compute ‖∑_{y_s} τ_st(y_s, y_t) − τ_t(y_t)‖_1 across edges. If violations are large, the theoretical link to belief propagation marginals breaks down, undermining the proxy construction. At minimum, they should demonstrate that constraint violations are small relative to label probabilities.

- Generalization under structural distribution shift untested: The inductive setting assumes test graphs may differ from training graphs in topology and label dependencies. However, all experiments use graphs from the same domain (e.g., citation networks). The model's reliance on learned edge pseudomarginals assumes label co-occurrence patterns generalize, but this is unverified. The authors should test SPN on a synthetic variant of PPI or Cora* where edge label correlations are perturbed in test graphs (e.g., flipping dependency directions or reducing homophily). If performance degrades sharply, the method is sensitive to structural assumptions, limiting its practical applicability. Such a test is standard for inductive benchmarks and necessary to support generalization claims.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide empirical validation of the proxy objective’s approximation quality, e.g., by comparing log-likelihoods or marginal accuracy before and after refinement.

- Address the pseudomarginal consistency constraints—either by enforcing them during training or demonstrating their empirical satisfaction.

- Include an ablation comparing SPN to a non-CRF model that also learns edge label predictions, to isolate the benefit of structured inference.

- Evaluate model robustness to shifts in label dependency structure between training and test graphs.

- Report inference time and convergence behavior of belief propagation on test graphs to support scalability claims.

comments.title: Moment-matching conditions misstated
comments.quote:
The optimal  $\theta$ -functions are characterized by the following moment-matching conditions:

$p_{\theta}(y_{s}|\mathbf{x}_{V},E)=\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\} \quad \forall s\in V, \quad p_{\theta}(y_{s},y_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{V},E)=\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\} \quad \forall (s,t)\in E,$ (4)
comments.explanation: The moment-matching conditions in Eq. (4) are incorrectly stated. The optimal solution to the maximin problem requires that the model marginals match the empirical marginals, not delta functions at the observed labels. The correct conditions should be $p_{\theta}(y_s | \mathbf{x}_V, E) = \hat{p}(y_s)$ and $p_{\theta}(y_s, y_t | \mathbf{x}_V, E) = \hat{p}(y_s, y_t)$, where $\hat{p}$ is the empirical distribution defined by the training labels. In the fully observed case, this reduces to indicator functions, but the current notation suggests a pointwise equality of distributions rather than marginal matching, which misrepresents the principle of maximum likelihood in exponential families.

comments.title: Incorrect bound direction in maximin game
comments.quote: On the one hand, learning relies on inference, meaning that we have to update  $\{q_s(y_s)\}_{s\in V}$ ,  $\{q_{st}(y_s,y_t)\}_{(s,t)\in E}$  to approximate the node and edge marginals of  $p_\theta$  at each step, which can be expensive. On the other hand, as learning involves a maximin game and the optimal  $q$  of the inner minimization problem in Eq. (3) is intractable, we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for  $\theta$ , making learning unstable.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for $\theta$' is incorrect. In the maximin formulation (Eq. 3), $\mathcal{L}(\theta, q)$ is a lower bound on $\log p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_V^*)$, not an upper bound. This is because the negative entropy term ensures $\min_q \mathcal{L}(\theta, q) \leq \log p_\theta(\mathbf{y}_V^*)$, with equality when $q = p_\theta$. Therefore, maximizing $\min_q \mathcal{L}(\theta, q)$ corresponds to maximizing a lower bound—the standard ELBO in variational inference. Calling it an 'upper bound' mischaracterizes the optimization and could confuse readers about convergence and model selection.

comments.title: Pseudomarginal consistency not enforced or verified
comments.quote: The last consistency constraint (i.e. $\sum_{y_{s}}\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})=\tau_{t}(y_{t})$ and $\sum_{y_{t}}\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})=\tau_{s}(y_{s})$) can be ignored during optimization, since by optimizing the objective function, the optimal pseudomarginals $\tau$ should well approximate the observed node and edge marginals, i.e., $\tau_{s}(y_{s})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ and $\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\}$, and hence $\tau$ will almost naturally satisfy the consistency constraint.
comments.explanation: The claim that pseudomarginals will 'almost naturally satisfy' the consistency constraints is implausible and unsupported. The node and edge GNNs are trained independently, with no mechanism to enforce $\sum_{y_s} \tau_{st}(y_s,y_t) = \tau_t(y_t)$. Even if both sides approximate one-hot vectors, there is no guarantee they align. Proposition 1 requires these constraints for the fixed-point correspondence with belief propagation marginals. Without constraint satisfaction, the theoretical justification breaks down. The paper should either enforce consistency (e.g., via normalization) or report empirical violations on a validation set to assess the approximation error.

comments.title: Refinement contradicts near-optimality claim
comments.quote: By solving the proxy problem, we can obtain a near-optimal joint distribution. In practice, we observe that when we have a large amount of training data, further refining this joint distribution by solving the maximin game in Eq. (3) for a few iterations can lead to further improvement.
comments.explanation: The term 'near-optimal' suggests the solution is close to the global optimum, implying refinement should yield minimal gains. Yet the authors observe consistent improvements from refinement, especially in high-data regimes (Table 4). This indicates the proxy solution is systematically suboptimal, not just statistically noisy. If refinement corrects meaningful biases—e.g., due to inconsistent pseudomarginals or uncalibrated potentials—then the proxy does not achieve its stated goal of avoiding costly inference while preserving optimality. The contradiction weakens the theoretical motivation.

comments.title: Edge pseudomarginal symmetry not enforced
comments.quote: $\{\mathbf{v}_{s}\}_{s\in V}=\mathrm{GNN}_{\text{edge}}(\mathbf{x}_{V},E)\qquad\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})=\mathrm{softmax}(g(\mathbf{v}_{s},\mathbf{v}_{t}))[y_{s},y_{t}],$ (8)
comments.explanation: The edge pseudomarginal $\tau_{st}(y_s,y_t)$ is defined via $g(\mathbf{v}_s, \mathbf{v}_t)$, but the paper does not require $g$ to be symmetric. However, $\tau_{st}(y_s,y_t)$ must equal $\tau_{ts}(y_t,y_s)$ for consistency with an undirected graph. If $g$ is asymmetric (e.g., a feedforward network with ordered inputs), then $\tau_{st}(y_s,y_t) \neq \tau_{ts}(y_t,y_s)$, violating the symmetry of the underlying Markov network. This leads to two different potentials for the same edge, breaking model coherence. The paper should enforce symmetry via a permutation-invariant $g$ or by averaging $g(\mathbf{v}_s, \mathbf{v}_t)$ and $g(\mathbf{v}_t, \mathbf{v}_s)$.

comments.title: Index mismatch in inference rule
comments.quote: the best label $y_{s}^{*}$ for each test node $\hat{s}\in\hat{V}$ should be predicted as $y_{s}^{*}=\arg\max_{y_{\hat{s}}}p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{t}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$, where $p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{t}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ is the marginal label distribution of node $\hat{s}$ induced by the joint $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$.
comments.explanation: The expression $\arg\max_{y_{\hat{s}}}p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{t}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ contains a mismatched index: it maximizes over $y_{\hat{s}}$ but references $y_{\hat{t}}$ in the probability. This is logically inconsistent—the marginal distribution should be over the label of node $\hat{s}$, not $\hat{t}$. Given that this defines the core inference rule, the error affects correctness. The correct form must reference $y_{\hat{s}}$ inside the probability.

comments.title: Max-product decoding without backtracking is invalid
comments.quote: This problem can be approximately solved by the max-product variant of loopy belief propagation, which simply replaces the sum over $y_{\hat{t}}$ in Eq. (12) with max *(Weiss & Freeman, 2001)*. Afterwards, the best node label can be still decoded via Eq. (13).
comments.explanation: Replacing the sum with max in Eq. (12) does not yield a valid max-product belief propagation update for recovering the full MAP assignment. While the message update is correct, decoding via Eq. (13) independently per node does not guarantee a globally consistent labeling. Max-product LBP requires backtracking to recover the joint assignment $\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}^*$, not just taking the argmax of individual marginals. The claim that Eq. (13) suffices for graph-level MAP inference is therefore incorrect and could mislead readers about the decoding procedure.

comments.title: Graph-level accuracy definition is inconsistent with practice
comments.quote: The graph-level accuracy measures *how likely a model can correctly classify all the nodes for a given test graph*.
comments.explanation: This definition implies graph-level accuracy is the probability that *all* nodes in a test graph are correctly classified—a strict joint accuracy. However, given that test graphs in Cora* etc. contain multiple nodes, this metric would be nearly zero even for strong models. The reported results (e.g., 60% in Table 2) suggest a different interpretation, possibly mean node accuracy per graph. The definition must be corrected to match what is actually computed. Rewrite as: 'Graph-level accuracy is the fraction of test graphs in which all node labels are predicted correctly' if perfect match is intended, or revise to reflect average node accuracy if that is the case.

comments.title: CRF-GNN training details underspecified
comments.quote: We train these models via the maximin game as in Eq. (3) with sum-product loopy belief propagation.
comments.explanation: The statement claims CRF-GNN variants are trained using sum-product loopy belief propagation, but this creates a mismatch in inference and learning objectives. The maximin game requires computing marginals via sum-product, which is expensive and contradicts the motivation for SPN. Yet no training time, convergence details, or approximation level (e.g., number of BP iterations) are provided. Without clarification, the comparison may be unfair: CRF-GNN could be handicapped by expensive inference while SPN avoids it. The paper should specify whether sum-product is run approximately, how many iterations are used, and whether marginals are computed per epoch or step.

comments.title: CRF-linear features undefined
comments.quote: This variant uses linear $\theta$-functions in Eq. (2), which takes the features on nodes and edges for computation.
comments.explanation: The description of CRF-linear assumes access to 'features on nodes and edges' but does not specify what these features are. If they are raw input features, this variant may be severely disadvantaged compared to GNN-based methods that learn expressive representations. Conversely, if the features are GNN-derived, then CRF-linear becomes a hybrid model and the comparison is not clean. The paper must clarify whether CRF-linear uses handcrafted features or learned embeddings. Add a sentence specifying the exact feature sources, such as 'using raw node attributes and one-hot edge indicators', to ensure reproducibility and fairness.

comments.title: Inference cost not discussed despite efficiency claims
comments.quote: SPNs are much more efficient, because the proxy optimization problem in SPNs is much easier to solve.
comments.explanation: The efficiency claim focuses solely on training time (Table 3), but ignores inference cost, which is critical in the inductive setting. If both SPN and CRF use loopy belief propagation for inference, SPN inherits the same high inference cost as CRFs, while standard GNNs infer in a single forward pass. The paper does not report inference time or LBP convergence speed, making the efficiency claim misleading. For fair comparison, inference cost should be included—especially since GNNs are typically valued for fast inference.

comments.title: Future work mischaracterizes current model
comments.quote: In the future, we plan to explore more advanced GNNs to model the pseudomargins on edges, which are key to improving node classification results in SPNs.
comments.explanation: This statement incorrectly implies that the current SPN model does not already use GNNs to model pseudomarginals on edges. However, Section 4.1 explicitly defines $\tau_{st}(y_s,y_t)$ via an edge GNN. Therefore, the use of GNNs for edge pseudomarginals is not future work but a core component of the current method. The claim misrepresents the existing architecture and suggests a development that has already been implemented.

comments.title: GNN label dependency modeling misrepresented
comments.quote: However, one limitation of GNNs is that they ignore the joint dependency of node labels, and therefore node labels are predicted separately without modeling structured output.
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents modern GNNs, which inherently model dependencies between node labels through message passing. By aggregating features from neighbors, GNNs capture local graph structure and implicitly account for label correlations—especially in homophilous graphs. The statement suggests GNNs make independent predictions, but in practice, the representation of each node depends on its neighborhood, meaning label predictions are not made in isolation. The paper should clarify that the limitation is not the absence of dependency modeling, but the lack of explicit probabilistic modeling of joint label configurations.

comments.title: Overgeneralization to non-graph structured tasks
comments.quote: In practice, many structured prediction problems can be viewed as special cases of inductive node classification, where the graphs between nodes have some special structures. For example in sequence labeling tasks (e.g., named entity recognition), the graphs between nodes have sequential structures. Thus, SPN can be applied to these tasks as well.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'many structured prediction problems' are special cases of inductive node classification is too strong and lacks justification. Inductive node classification assumes generalization across graphs, while sequence labeling is typically a transductive or single-instance prediction task. Applying SPN to such tasks would require reinterpreting sequences as graphs and assuming shared dependency structures across different sequences—something not supported by the current model design or experiments. The discussion incorrectly conflates structural similarity with the inductive setting.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Structured Proxy Networks (SPNs) combining GNNs and CRFs for inductive node classification. The core contribution is a proxy optimization problem that avoids the costly maximin game in standard CRF training. While the method shows empirical improvements, there are significant concerns about the theoretical justification of the proxy optimization, the evaluation methodology, and the scope of claims.

The paper presents an interesting approach to combining GNNs with structured prediction, addressing a real challenge in CRF training. The empirical results on multiple datasets are promising, showing consistent improvements over both GNN and CRF baselines. However, the theoretical foundation appears incomplete, and the evaluation has some limitations that temper confidence in the claims.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical justification for proxy optimization is incomplete: Proposition 1 claims that parameterizing θ-functions using pseudomarginals τ yields a fixed point of loopy belief propagation, but the proof (referenced to Sec C) is not provided in the main text. More importantly, the paper does not establish theoretical bounds on how far the proxy solution can be from the true optimum of the maximin game. The claim that the proxy yields a "near-optimal" solution is supported only empirically (Table 5), without theoretical analysis of conditions under which the approximation might fail. This is critical because the entire method rests on this approximation being reasonable in practice.

- Inconsistent evaluation between node-level and graph-level settings: The paper evaluates two different settings (node-level vs graph-level accuracy) but uses different metrics and datasets, making direct comparison difficult. For node-level (PPI, DBLP), they report accuracy and micro-F1, while for graph-level (Cora*, Citeseer*, Pubmed*), they report percentage of perfectly classified graphs. This graph-level metric is extremely stringent and may not reflect practical performance. More concerning, the relative improvements between settings differ substantially - SPN shows large gains in PPI but only modest improvements in Cora* (60.47% vs 59.15% for GCNII). This suggests the method's effectiveness may be dataset-dependent, which isn't adequately discussed.

- Training protocol for CRF baselines may be suboptimal: The CRF-GNN baselines are trained using the maximin game with sum-product belief propagation, while SPN uses max-product by default (Sec 5.4). This creates an unfair comparison since max-product is generally more suitable for MAP inference tasks. Additionally, Table 3 shows CRF training takes 50-100x longer than SPN, but it's unclear whether this is due to the optimization method or implementation details. The paper should either (a) use identical inference algorithms for fair comparison, or (b) demonstrate that the chosen algorithms are optimal for each method.

- Limited analysis of inductive generalization capabilities: The paper claims to address the inductive setting but provides limited analysis of how well the model generalizes to truly novel graph structures. The PPI variants (PPI-1, PPI-2, PPI-10) test generalization across different amounts of training data from the same distribution, while the ego-network construction for Cora*/Citeseer*/Pubmed* creates test graphs with similar structure to training graphs. There's no evaluation on graphs with fundamentally different topologies or feature distributions, which is the core challenge of inductive learning mentioned in the introduction.

- Ablation study missing for key architectural choices: The paper uses separate node and edge GNNs by default but shows in Table 6 that a shared GNN performs similarly. However, there's no systematic ablation studying: (1) the impact of edge GNN capacity on performance, (2) the effect of the annealing temperature γ, (3) how sensitive performance is to the choice of divergence measure d in Eq. 9, or (4) why SPN-GCN underperforms relative to SPN-GAT (mentioned in Sec G.1 but not shown). These analyses are crucial for understanding the method's design space and practical deployment.

- Missing worked example of proxy optimization: The paper introduces a novel proxy optimization problem but never demonstrates it on a concrete, fully-specified model. Readers need to see how the pseudomarginals τ are computed and combined to form θ-functions for a specific graph structure. A small synthetic example—perhaps a 3-node graph with binary labels—would clarify the mechanics. Show the node and edge GNN outputs, the resulting τ values, the constructed θ-functions via Eq. (5), and verify that the resulting joint distribution satisfies the moment-matching conditions approximately. This is essential because the entire method hinges on this construction being intuitive and correct.

- No demonstration of proxy optimality gap: The paper claims the proxy optimization yields a 'near-optimal' solution but provides no characterization of when this approximation might fail. A minimal simulation showing the gap between proxy and true maximin solutions would strengthen the paper. Consider a simple graph family (e.g., chains or grids) where the maximin game can be solved exactly via dynamic programming. Compare the proxy solution against the true optimum across varying graph sizes or noise levels. This would establish the boundaries of the method's effectiveness and address the domain-specific concern about conditions under which the approximation fails.

- Implementation guidance for high-dimensional settings missing: For practical adoption, the paper needs to discuss how SPN scales with graph size and label space dimensionality. The edge GNN computes a |Y|×|Y| matrix for each edge, which becomes prohibitive for large label sets (e.g., PPI has 121 labels). The paper should analyze computational complexity and provide practical recommendations. For instance, discuss whether low-rank factorization of edge potentials or sparse label dependencies can mitigate this issue. Without this guidance, practitioners cannot assess feasibility for real-world applications with many classes.

- Underdeveloped generalization analysis for inductive setting: The paper's inductive evaluation (Sec 5.1) tests generalization across data splits but not across graph structure distributions. To fully address the inductive challenge mentioned in the introduction, include experiments where test graphs have different topologies than training graphs. For example, train on scale-free networks and test on small-world graphs, or vary average degree distributions. This would demonstrate whether SPN truly generalizes to unseen graph structures or merely interpolates within the same distribution, which is crucial for the paper's stated contribution.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a complete theoretical analysis of the proxy optimization, including bounds on approximation quality and conditions under which it may fail

- Standardize evaluation metrics across settings and add analysis of why performance varies between node-level and graph-level tasks

- Ensure fair comparison with CRF baselines by using consistent inference algorithms and providing more detailed training protocol

- Add experiments testing generalization to graphs with different structural properties from training graphs

- Include comprehensive ablation studies for architectural hyperparameters and design choices

comments.title: Moment-matching conditions are incorrect
comments.quote:
The optimal  $\theta$ -functions are characterized by the following moment-matching conditions:

$$
p _ {\theta} \left(y _ {s} \mid \mathbf {x} _ {V}, E\right) = \mathbb {I} _ {y _ {s} ^ {*}} \left\{y _ {s} \right\} \quad \forall s \in V, \quad p _ {\theta} \left(y _ {s}, y _ {t} \mid \mathbf {x} _ {V}, E\right) = \mathbb {I} _ {\left(y _ {s} ^ {*}, y _ {t} ^ {*}\right)} \left\{\left(y _ {s}, y _ {t}\right) \right\} \quad \forall (s, t) \in E, \tag {4}
$$
comments.explanation: Taking the derivative of Eq. (3) with respect to θ_s(y_s) and setting to zero yields: ∂L/∂θ_s(y_s) = I_{y_s^*}{y_s} - q_s(y_s) = 0, so q_s(y_s) = I_{y_s^*}{y_s}. Similarly for edges: ∂L/∂θ_st(y_s,y_t) = I_{(y_s^*,y_t^*)}{(y_s,y_t)} - q_st(y_s,y_t) = 0, so q_st(y_s,y_t) = I_{(y_s^*,y_t^*)}{(y_s,y_t)}. The optimal conditions match the variational distribution q to the empirical distribution, not the model distribution p_θ. Rewrite Eq. (4) as q_s(y_s) = I_{y_s^*}{y_s} and q_st(y_s,y_t) = I_{(y_s^*,y_t^*)}{(y_s,y_t)} because the derivative of the loss with respect to θ yields conditions on q, not p_θ.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about maximizing upper bound
comments.quote: On the other hand, as learning involves a maximin game and the optimal  $q$  of the inner minimization problem in Eq. (3) is intractable, we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for  $\theta$ , making learning unstable.
comments.explanation: In the maximin formulation max_θ min_q L(θ,q), since min_q L(θ,q) ≤ L(θ,q) for any q, we have max_θ min_q L(θ,q) ≤ max_θ L(θ,q). The inner minimization provides a lower bound on the likelihood, not an upper bound. When we use an approximate q instead of the true minimizer, we get max_θ L(θ,q_approx) ≥ max_θ min_q L(θ,q), meaning we're maximizing a looser upper bound, but this is due to approximation, not the maximin formulation itself. Rewrite the sentence as 'we can only maximize a lower bound of the likelihood function for θ when using the exact minimizer, and an upper bound when using approximate q, making learning unstable' because the maximin formulation itself provides a lower bound.

comments.title: Incorrect factorization assumption in GNNs
comments.quote:
GNNs assume all node labels are independent conditioned on node features and edges, so the joint label distribution is factorized into a set of marginals as below:

$p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{V}|\mathbf{x}_{V},E)=\prod_{s\in V}p_{\theta}(y_{s}|\mathbf{x}_{V},E).$ (1)
comments.explanation: Equation (1) does not accurately represent how GNNs work. GNNs use message passing to create node representations that incorporate information from neighbors, creating dependencies between node predictions through the shared computation graph. While the final predictions may be computed independently, the representations themselves encode inter-node dependencies. The factorization in (1) would only hold for truly independent predictions, which is not the case for GNNs. Rewrite the passage as: "GNNs compute node predictions independently given learned representations, but these representations are obtained through message passing that incorporates information from neighboring nodes and edges. While the final prediction step can be viewed as computing marginals, the underlying representations capture inter-node dependencies through the shared message passing process."

comments.title: Unjustified dismissal of consistency constraints
comments.quote: The last consistency constraint (i.e. $\sum_{y_{s}}\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})=\tau_{t}(y_{t})$ and $\sum_{y_{t}}\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})=\tau_{s}(y_{s})$) can be ignored during optimization, since by optimizing the objective function, the optimal pseudomarginals $\tau$ should well approximate the observed node and edge marginals, i.e., $\tau_{s}(y_{s})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ and $\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\}$, and hence $\tau$ will almost naturally satisfy the consistency constraint.
comments.explanation:
The justification for ignoring consistency constraints is mathematically incomplete. While perfect approximation of indicator functions would guarantee consistency (since $\sum_{y_{s}}\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\}=\mathbb{I}_{y_{t}^{*}}\{y_{t}\}$), neural network approximations are imperfect. Consider a counterexample where $\tau_{s}(y_{s})$ and $\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})$ are learned independently: they could both approximate their respective targets well while violating consistency. The paper needs either a theoretical bound on consistency violation or empirical evidence that the optimization implicitly enforces it.

Add a sentence after the quoted text: "While perfect approximation would guarantee consistency, in practice we observe that the neural network optimization implicitly enforces approximate consistency, as verified in our empirical analysis (see Sec. D)." because this acknowledges the approximation issue while providing empirical justification.

comments.title: Variable inconsistency in marginal prediction formula
comments.quote: Intuitively, the best label $y_{s}^{*}$ for each test node $\hat{s}\in\hat{V}$ should be predicted as $y_{s}^{*}=\arg\max_{y_{\hat{s}}}p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{t}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$, where $p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{t}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ is the marginal label distribution of node $\hat{s}$ induced by the joint $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$.
comments.explanation: The formula contains a variable mismatch: it predicts the label for node $\hat{s}$ using the marginal distribution of node $\hat{t}$. The argmax is over $y_{\hat{s}}$ but the marginal is $p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{t}}|\cdot)$. This should be $p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{s}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ to be consistent. Rewrite the sentence as: "Intuitively, the best label $y_{\hat{s}}^{*}$ for each test node $\hat{s}\in\hat{V}$ should be predicted as $y_{\hat{s}}^{*}=\arg\max_{y_{\hat{s}}}p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{s}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$, where $p_{\theta}(y_{\hat{s}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ is the marginal label distribution of node $\hat{s}$ induced by the joint $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$."

comments.title: Ambiguous decoding rule for max-product variant
comments.quote: This problem can be approximately solved by the max-product variant of loopy belief propagation, which simply replaces the sum over $y_{\hat{t}}$ in Eq. (12) with max *(Weiss & Freeman, 2001)*. Afterwards, the best node label can be still decoded via Eq. (13).
comments.explanation: The decoding rule for max-product BP is not the same as for sum-product BP. Equation (13) is derived for marginal inference (sum-product), but for max-product BP, the correct decoding should track maximizing assignments during message passing. The current text incorrectly suggests using the same decoding. Replace with: "Afterwards, the best node label can be decoded by backtracking from the maximizing assignments during message passing, or equivalently by computing $y_{\hat{s}}^{*}=\arg\max_{y_{\hat{s}}}[\exp(\theta_{\hat{s}}(y_{\hat{s}}))\prod_{\hat{t}\in N(\hat{s})}m_{\hat{t}\rightarrow\hat{s}}^{\text{max}}(y_{\hat{s}})]$ where $m^{\text{max}}$ are max-product messages."

comments.title: Inconsistent CRF training protocols
comments.quote: We train these models via the maximin game as in Eq. (3) with sum-product loopy belief propagation.
comments.explanation: The section states that CRF-GNN models are trained using sum-product belief propagation, but Sec 5.4 indicates SPN uses max-product by default. This creates an unfair comparison since max-product is generally more suitable for MAP inference tasks. The inconsistency matters because it could explain the large performance differences reported. Either use identical inference algorithms for fair comparison, or explicitly justify why different algorithms are optimal for each method.

comments.title: DBLP dataset construction lacks clarity
comments.quote: Papers from eight conferences are treated as nodes, and we split them into three categories for classification according to conference domains . For each paper, we compute the mean GloVe embedding *(Pennington et al., 2014)* of words in the title and abstract as node features. The training/validation/test graph is formed as the citation graph of papers published before 1999, from 2000 to 2009, after 2010 respectively.
comments.explanation: The DBLP dataset description has two gaps. First, it mentions splitting papers into "three categories for classification according to conference domains" but doesn't specify what these categories are or which conferences belong to each category. This is essential for understanding the classification task. Second, the temporal split creates three separate graphs (training, validation, test), but it's unclear how citation edges are handled across time boundaries - can a paper in the training set cite a paper in the test set? This affects the inductive nature of the evaluation. Add the specific conference categories and clarify the cross-time citation handling.

comments.title: Ego network construction method unspecified
comments.quote: For each training/validation/test node of the raw dataset, we treat its ego network as a training/validation/test graph. We denote the datasets as Cora*, Citeseer*, Pubmed*.
comments.explanation: The ego network construction is underspecified. An ego network requires defining the radius (how many hops from the ego node to include) and whether the ego node itself is included in the resulting graph. These choices significantly impact graph size and structure, which affects the inductive evaluation. Without this specification, the results cannot be reproduced. State the exact ego network parameters used, e.g., "we use 1-hop ego networks including the ego node and all its neighbors".

comments.title: Inconsistent experimental design across datasets
comments.quote: For Cora*, Citeseer*, and Pubmed*, we run each compared method with 10 different seeds to report the mean accuracy and the standard deviation. For DBLP and PPI, we run each method with 5 seeds.
comments.explanation: The experimental design is inconsistent across datasets, using 10 seeds for graph-level datasets (Cora*, Citeseer*, Pubmed*) but only 5 seeds for node-level datasets (DBLP, PPI). This inconsistency makes statistical comparisons unreliable since the variance estimates are based on different sample sizes. The standard deviations reported for the graph-level datasets will be more stable than those for node-level datasets simply due to having twice as many random seeds. Rewrite the experimental protocol to use the same number of seeds across all datasets, preferably 10 seeds for consistency and better statistical reliability.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about SPN applicability
comments.quote: In practice, many structured prediction problems can be viewed as special cases of inductive node classification, where the graphs between nodes have some special structures. For example in sequence labeling tasks (e.g., named entity recognition), the graphs between nodes have sequential structures. Thus, SPN can be applied to these tasks as well.
comments.explanation: The claim that SPN can be directly applied to sequence labeling tasks is not supported by the paper's contributions. SPN was designed and evaluated specifically for graph-structured data with arbitrary connectivity, not sequential structures. The paper provides no theoretical or empirical evidence that the proxy optimization approach works effectively on linear chain graphs, nor does it address how the edge GNN components would handle the simpler sequential structure. This overgeneralization should be tempered or backed by specific analysis.

comments.title: Typographical error in marginal approximation
comments.quote: Combining these two sets of formula results in $p_{\theta}(y_{s})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ and $p_{\theta}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$.
comments.explanation:
The second approximation contains a clear typographical error. The right-hand side $\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ depends only on $y_{s}$, while the left-hand side $p_{\theta}(y_{s},y_{t})$ is a joint distribution over both $y_{s}$ and $y_{t}$. Based on the preceding derivation where $\tau_{st}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\}$, the correct approximation should be $p_{\theta}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\}$ to maintain consistency with the joint indicator function used throughout.

Rewrite the sentence as: "Combining these two sets of formula results in $p_{\theta}(y_{s})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ and $p_{\theta}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*},y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s},y_{t})\}$." because the joint marginal should approximate the joint indicator function, not just the node indicator.

comments.title: Incorrect marginal distribution notation in max-product setting
comments.quote: In this case, the best prediction of node labels is given by $\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}^{*}=\arg\max_{\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}}p(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$.
comments.explanation: The notation $p(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ is inconsistent with the earlier notation $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$ used throughout the section. Since this is still using the same learned $\theta$-functions, the subscript $\theta$ should be retained for consistency. Rewrite as: "In this case, the best prediction of node labels is given by $\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}^{*}=\arg\max_{\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}}p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\hat{V}}|\mathbf{x}_{\hat{V}},\hat{E})$."

comments.title: Table 4 labeling inconsistency
comments.explanation: The table has inconsistent algorithm labeling. The first and third rows both show 'SPN-' but these should be SPN-GAT and SPN-GraphSAGE respectively to match the paired GAT and GraphSAGE rows. This makes the table ambiguous - it's unclear which SPN variant corresponds to which baseline GNN. The text mentions 'SPN-GAT' and 'SPN-GraphSAGE' in the surrounding discussion, so the table should explicitly label these rows for clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent PPI dataset description
comments.quote: We use the PPI dataset *(Zitnik and Leskovec, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017)*, which has 20 training graphs. To make the dataset more challenging, we also try using only the first 1/2/10 training graphs, yielding another three datasets PPI-1, PPI-2, and PPI-10.
comments.explanation: The description contains an inconsistency. The text states the PPI dataset has 20 training graphs, but then creates variants PPI-1, PPI-2, and PPI-10 using only the first 1/2/10 training graphs. The slash notation "1/2/10" is ambiguous - it could mean fractions (1/20, 2/20, 10/20) or the numbers 1, 2, and 10. Given that PPI-10 is mentioned, this likely means using the first 1, 2, and 10 graphs respectively. The notation should be clarified to avoid confusion. Rewrite "the first 1/2/10 training graphs" as "the first 1, 2, and 10 training graphs respectively" to make the meaning unambiguous.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes the Structured Proxy Network (SPN), an inductive node classification method that parameterizes CRF potential functions using GNNs. To avoid the computational burden of the standard maximin optimization for CRFs, the authors introduce a proxy objective based on pseudomarginal consistency, which allows for efficient training via standard GNN optimization techniques.

The paper makes a clear and well-motivated contribution by bridging GNNs and structured prediction for the inductive setting. The proposed proxy objective effectively bypasses the intractability of joint inference during training, and the experimental results demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines. The approach is both novel and practically relevant for graph machine learning.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Justification of the Proxy Objective: Proposition 1 claims that the proxy objective leads to a near-optimal joint distribution by satisfying moment-matching conditions. However, the paper does not explicitly bound the approximation error introduced by ignoring the consistency constraints during the optimization of Eq. (9). Since the consistency constraint is only 'roughly' satisfied by the proxy, it is unclear how this error propagates to the final joint distribution $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{V}|\mathbf{x}_{V},E)$. A more rigorous discussion or a small-scale synthetic experiment demonstrating the gap between the proxy-optimized parameters and the true maximin solution would strengthen the theoretical foundation.

- Sensitivity to Graph Heterophily: The methodology relies on the assumption that GNN-based potentials can effectively capture label dependencies. In disassortative or heterophilic graphs, where neighboring nodes often have different labels, the pair-wise potential $\theta_{st}(y_s, y_t)$ becomes critical. The paper does not analyze how the performance of SPN degrades when the graph structure provides misleading signals about label correlations. Including an evaluation on known heterophilic benchmarks (e.g., Chameleon or Squirrel) would clarify the robustness of the structured prediction component.

- Scalability and Inference Costs: While the authors highlight the efficiency of the proxy learning phase, the inference phase still relies on loopy belief propagation (Eq. 12). The paper lacks a detailed complexity analysis regarding the convergence of this inference scheme on large, dense test graphs. Given that loopy belief propagation is not guaranteed to converge on graphs with complex loop structures, the paper should provide empirical evidence on the number of iterations required for convergence across the different datasets and the impact of non-convergence on final accuracy.

- Ablation of the Edge GNN Component: The SPN architecture employs both a node GNN and an edge GNN. It is currently difficult to discern how much of the performance gain is due to the structured prediction framework (the CRF component) versus the additional capacity provided by the edge GNN. To isolate the contribution of the structured prediction, the authors should report results for a baseline that uses the same edge-based features but performs independent classification, effectively removing the CRF inference step.

- Need for a controlled synthetic experiment on approximation error: The paper relies on the claim that the proxy objective (Eq. 9) yields a near-optimal joint distribution. However, the theoretical link between the proxy solution and the true maximin solution remains qualitative. A synthetic experiment on a small, fully-specified grid graph where the ground truth joint distribution is computable would demonstrate the magnitude of the approximation error. Specifically, the authors should compute the gap between the proxy-optimized $\theta$ and the exact maximin $\theta$ to verify that the proposed approximation does not introduce significant bias.

- Comparison with a non-structured edge-feature baseline: The performance gains of the SPN are attributed to the combination of GNNs and CRFs. It is currently ambiguous whether the improvement stems from the structured inference (loopy belief propagation) or simply the additional capacity afforded by the edge GNN. To address this, the authors should include an 'Edge-GNN-Only' baseline that uses the same edge-based features for classification but ignores the CRF dependency structure. This would isolate the value added by the structured prediction component.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Include a discussion or empirical analysis regarding the approximation error of the proxy objective relative to the true maximin solution.

- Add results on at least one heterophilic graph dataset to demonstrate the robustness of the structured prediction component in non-homophilic settings.

- Provide a brief analysis of the convergence behavior of loopy belief propagation on the test sets, including the average number of iterations to convergence.

- Perform an ablation study that isolates the benefit of the CRF inference step from the capacity of the edge GNN.

comments.title: Ambiguity in moment-matching conditions
comments.quote:
$\theta$ -functions are characterized by the following moment-matching conditions:

$$
p _ {\theta} \left(y _ {s} \mid \mathbf {x} _ {V}, E\right) = \mathbb {I} _ {y _ {s} ^ {*}} \left\{y _ {s} \right\} \quad \forall s \in V, \quad p _ {\theta} \left(y _ {s}, y _ {t} \mid \mathbf {x} _ {V}, E\right) = \mathbb {I} _ {\left(y _ {s} ^ {*}, y _ {t} ^ {*}\right)} \left\{\left(y _ {s}, y _ {t}\right) \right\} \quad \forall (s, t) \in E, \tag {4}
$$

where  $\
comments.explanation: The moment-matching conditions in Eq. (4) imply that the model $p_{\theta}$ must place all probability mass on the ground truth labels. This is only possible if the model is perfectly expressive and the data is noise-free. In practice, for a CRF with finite parameters, the marginals of $p_{\theta}$ will generally not be indicator functions of the ground truth. Please clarify that these conditions represent the limit as the model approaches the empirical distribution, or specify the conditions on the parameter space that allow for this convergence.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance claims for SPN-GCN
comments.quote: Table 1: Result on PPI datasets (in %). SPNs get consistent improvement on GNNs and CRFs.
comments.explanation: The claim that SPNs provide consistent improvement over GNNs is contradicted by the data in Table 1 for the GCN backbone. For example, on PPI-1, the GCN Micro-F1 is 54.55 ± 0.29, while SPN-GCN is 54.15 ± 0.17. Similarly, on PPI-10, GCN achieves 62.48 ± 0.27 Micro-F1, while SPN-GCN achieves 61.36 ± 0.11. Please revise the text to acknowledge that the structured prediction component does not consistently improve performance across all metrics and datasets.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in joint distribution approximation
comments.quote: Combining these two sets of formula results in $p_{\theta}(y_{s})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ and $p_{\theta}(y_{s},y_{t})\approx\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$.
comments.explanation: The second approximation is notationally inconsistent. The joint distribution $p_{\theta}(y_{s}, y_{t})$ is a function of two variables, whereas the indicator function $\mathbb{I}_{y_{s}^{*}}\{y_{s}\}$ only depends on the label of node $s$. The correct target for the joint distribution is the joint indicator $\mathbb{I}_{(y_{s}^{*}, y_{t}^{*})}\{(y_{s}, y_{t})\}$. Please rewrite the second approximation to maintain consistency with the preceding derivation.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes the Structured Proxy Network (SPN) to combine GNNs and CRFs for inductive node classification. The method replaces the unstable maximin optimization of CRFs with a proxy problem that trains separate node and edge GNNs to match empirical label distributions. While the approach is practically efficient and shows empirical gains, the theoretical justification for the proxy, the choice of evaluation metrics, and the alignment between the inductive framing and dataset construction require substantial clarification.

The paper addresses a real pain point in graph machine learning: the optimization instability of CRF-GNN hybrids. The proxy formulation is intuitive and the experimental results demonstrate clear efficiency gains over direct maximin training. The work is well-motivated and the implementation is straightforward. However, the theoretical bridge between empirical marginal fitting and CRF moment-matching is underdeveloped, and several experimental design choices weaken the strength of the claims.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical gap between empirical marginals and CRF moment-matching: Section 4.1 derives a proxy problem that trains node and edge GNNs to maximize the log-likelihood of observed labels (Eq. 10). This forces the learned pseudomarginals to match the empirical one-hot labels on the training graph. The paper claims this satisfies the moment-matching conditions in Eq. (4), which require the model's own marginals to align with the empirical distribution. Standard CRF learning alternates between inference to compute model marginals and parameter updates to align them. By bypassing the partition function and directly fitting empirical labels, the proxy ignores the consistency constraints of the joint distribution. This matters because the resulting joint model may not be well-calibrated or may violate the Markov properties assumed in Eq. (2). The authors should either prove that minimizing empirical cross-entropy on nodes and edges asymptotically recovers the true CRF parameters under their architecture, or reframe the method as a pseudo-likelihood approximation rather than a moment-matching proxy.

- Graph-level accuracy metric obscures performance differences: Section 5.1 defines graph-level accuracy as the percentage of test graphs where all nodes are correctly classified. Table 2 shows this metric yields very low values across all methods on Cora*, Citeseer*, and Pubmed*. This metric is notoriously harsh for node classification and often saturates quickly, making it difficult to distinguish between models that differ by a few percentage points in node-level accuracy. It also penalizes models heavily for single-node errors, which may not reflect practical utility. The paper does not justify why this strict metric was chosen over standard node-level accuracy or macro-F1. I recommend reporting standard node-level metrics alongside the graph-level metric, or switching to a more granular measure like per-node accuracy or edge consistency scores to better demonstrate the structured prediction benefits.

- Misalignment between inductive framing and dataset construction: The introduction and abstract emphasize inductive node classification, which typically requires generalizing to entirely new graphs with unseen structures, features, or label distributions. However, the graph-level datasets are constructed by treating ego networks of a single transductive graph as separate training, validation, and test graphs. This setup tests local neighborhood prediction rather than true cross-graph inductive generalization. The PPI dataset is genuinely inductive, but the paper mixes these two fundamentally different settings under the same label. This framing limits the validity of the generalization claims. The authors should clearly separate the truly inductive PPI experiments from the local ego-network experiments, or explicitly test the model on completely unseen graph structures to substantiate the inductive claims.
